capabilities_used: ls/cat/find filesystem introspection of state dir
deferred file durability across kill -9 + restart re-arm
capabilities_attempted_but_failed: shellmux --help / help (no help system; dumps generic usage, exit 1)
shellmux ack / shellmux replay (no such subcommands — correctly absent, but no informative message either)
shellmux sub --help / pub --help (crash: 'line 323/342: $2: unbound variable' instead of usage)
evidence_log: CLI surface: `bash src/shellmux --help` / `help` / `ack` / `replay` all print `shellmux: usage: shellmux {serve|sub|pub} ... (got '<arg>')` and exit=1. No ack/replay subcommand exists; tool exposes ZERO delivery-guarantee surface — semantics are documentation-only.
BUG: `bash src/shellmux sub --help` -> `/work/src/shellmux: line 323: $2: unbound variable` exit=1; `pub --help` -> `line 342: $2: unbound variable` exit=1 (set -u leaks instead of usage).

Retained-message probe: `printf before-anyone-listens | pub D news` BEFORE any sub -> exit=0, creates topics/news/ dir but `find` shows NO message file. Later live sub got only `msg1-while-online`, never the pre-sub message. No last-value/retained.

Replay probe (Phase C/D): published offline-1/2/3 with NO subscriber -> `find $D -type f` shows ONLY broker.log + topics/news/.drops.lock (0 bytes). Reconnected subscriber's output [[[ ]]] EMPTY = recovers nothing. A subsequent live pub `after-reconnect-live` arrived, proving the sub works but got zero replay.
Forget-on-death: after `kill SUB`, ls topics/news/ shows the sub_*.fifo gone, only .drops.lock (a lock, not a buffer) remains.

Deferred-into-void: `pub D alerts --delay 2` with NO subscriber stages deferred file `1782179046477.246169763` (run_at_ms.seq); after deadline passes the deferred dir is EMPTY and topics/alerts/ has no leftover. A sub arriving after the deadline gets EMPTY output. Deferred = pending-deadline store, NOT a held/replayable message.
Fire-once: with a sub online, `--delay 2` fires exactly once -> `occurrences of fire-once: 1`, deferred/ empty after. No redelivery queue.
Modulo-crash Q2: ordinary publish then crash -> `find $D -type f ! -name broker.log` returns NOTHING. No durability for normal messages.

Modulo-crash Q1/Q3: `--delay 6` deferred file `1782179090039.2970621112` survived `kill -9` of broker+children (file still on disk while broker down); after restart + fresh sub it re-armed and fired with `occurrences: 1` (exactly once), deferred/ empty after. Only the pending-deadline record is durable, and at-most-once.

No-ack/no-retry: with a healthy sub, 200 serial publishes all delivered (`delivered count: 200`) and `deferred/` stayed EMPTY during fan-out (no retry queue staged). pub-to-empty-topic `exit=0 took=1.016s` (clean prompt no-op, just the linger).

Shipped tests/flood_wedged.sh (N_MAIN=200): result pass=4 fail=0; F2 `wedged-sub overflow is visible (sum drops_*=354), never silent` — dropped messages are COUNTED and DISCARDED, NOT retried/replayed; F3 max broker-descendants=21 (no writer pile-up).

Doc audit: DEMO.md line 116-117 `Not a serious broker. No persistence, acks, wildcards, auth/TLS... or clustering. At-most-once-modulo-crash, by design.` README line: deferred crash-recovery `documented as at-most-once-modulo-crash`. Observed behavior matches these claims exactly.

claim_audit.claim: DEMO.md L116-117: 'No persistence, acks... At-most-once-modulo-crash, by design.'
claim_audit.evidence: Ordinary publishes leave nothing on disk across a crash (find returns empty); offline subscribers recover nothing on reconnect; no ack/replay subcommand; only deferred-deadline records are durable and they fire at-most-once.
deferred file 1782179090039 survived kill -9, re-armed on restart, fired exactly once (occurrences:1).

claim_audit.claim: DEMO.md: backpressure is lossy but visible, never silent (drops_$pid)
claim_audit.evidence: flood_wedged.sh F2: sum drops_*=354 visible; dropped messages are not retried or queued for replay.

claim_audit.claim: No retained/last-value messages (README 'no retained messages' implied; persona note 'A subscriber must connect BEFORE you publish')
claim_audit.evidence: pub before any sub leaves no message; later sub never receives it.

proof_assessment.note: This challenge does not touch the deadline chaos proof directly, but it did exercise the deferred scheduler's crash-recovery and fire-once behavior as a USER. Observed: deferred file survives kill -9 and re-arms with exactly-once fire (occurrences:1); fire-once with a live sub also exactly-once. I did NOT run chaos_deadline.sh in this session (out of scope for the at-least-once probe), so I make no claim about the missed=0/dup=0/negative-control axis here.

what_worked_well: The tool is genuinely HONEST: every at-least-once / replay / ack probe came back negative, exactly matching DEMO.md's 'No persistence, acks... At-most-once-modulo-crash' disclaimer. A user is NOT misled.

The filesystem-as-API made this trivial to audit from outside: `find $STATE -type f` is the whole truth. There is no hidden message store — offline publishes leave literally nothing behind, which is the strongest possible non-misleading signal.

Deferred files are clearly a pending-DEADLINE store, not a durable message queue: they're consumed-on-fire, fire into the void with no subscriber, and don't replay to a late subscriber. The naming `<run_at_ms>.<seq>` even tells you it's a deadline, not a message log.

The one persistence corner ('modulo-crash') behaves precisely as documented: only the deferred record survives kill -9, and it re-arms and fires exactly once (occurrences:1) — at-most-once, not at-least-once.
Backpressure overload is counted-and-dropped (drops_*=354 in flood_wedged.sh), never silently retried — the integrator's exact wish: a visible, scriptable loss signal instead of hidden buffering.
wished_for: A one-line 'delivery semantics' note in the README body (not only DEMO.md's 'Why it's honest' footer) — a user who reads README first sees 'crash recovery' in the status line (L17) and could optimistically infer durability before reaching DEMO.md L116. Surface 'at-most-once, no replay/retain/ack' near the top of README.

A `--help` that actually prints the semantics caveat. Right now the binary itself says NOTHING about guarantees; the honesty lives only in docs. `shellmux --help` printing 'at-most-once; subscribers must connect before publish; no replay' would put the disclaimer where a scripter actually looks.

Fix `sub --help` / `pub --help` crashing with `$2: unbound variable` (src lines 323/342 per the error). A scripter probing the CLI hits an ugly set -u traceback instead of usage — bad first impression even though the underlying semantics are fine.

friction_points.description: The binary exposes ZERO information about delivery semantics. `shellmux --help`, `help`, and any bogus subcommand all dump the same `usage: shellmux {serve|sub|pub} ...` line and exit 1. A user evaluating delivery guarantees gets nothing from the tool itself and must find DEMO.md's 'Why it's honest' footer (L104-121). The honesty is real but lives only in prose a CLI user may never open.
friction_points.evidence: `bash src/shellmux --help` -> `shellmux: usage: ... (got '--help')` exit=1; no 'at-most-once' / 'no replay' anywhere in CLI output.
friction_points.workaround: Read DEMO.md L116-117 and the persona hint 'A subscriber must connect BEFORE you publish'.

friction_points.description: `shellmux sub --help` and `shellmux pub --help` crash with `line 323/342: $2: unbound variable` instead of printing usage. set -u leaks a raw bash error to a user who is just trying to discover options. Not specific to my challenge but directly in the path of a scripter probing for capabilities.
friction_points.evidence: `bash src/shellmux sub --help` -> `/work/src/shellmux: line 323: $2: unbound variable` exit=1; same at line 342 for pub.
friction_points.workaround: Don't pass --help to subcommands; consult docs.

friction_points.description: README L17 lists 'crash recovery' as a built feature in the status paragraph, and the repo map (L67) names tests/crash_recovery.sh, before the reader reaches the 'at-most-once-modulo-crash' qualifier (which is only in DEMO.md). An optimistic reader could infer message durability. The behavior is correct and honest once you read DEMO.md, but README's top-level framing leans on the strong word 'recovery' without the at-most-once qualifier nearby.
friction_points.evidence: README L17 'deferred delivery through that scheduler, forget-on-death, crash recovery, GC reaper'; qualifier 'At-most-once-modulo-crash' appears only in DEMO.md L117.

friction_points.description: Each `pub` invocation blocks ~1s (the linger) and spins a fresh bash+socat. A scripter's natural `for i in ...; do ... | shellmux pub ...; done` of 200 messages took 203s. Tangential to the at-least-once challenge, but it's the kind of integration friction a pipeline author hits immediately. (Not silent loss — all 200 were delivered.)
friction_points.evidence: noack2 probe: '200 publishes took: 203.32s', delivered count 200; pub-to-empty 'took=1.016s'.
friction_points.workaround: Keep a long-lived publisher connection / batch, or accept the linger; for high-rate use a real broker.

free_text:
VERDICT: shellmux PASSES this challenge. It is honest — there is no at-least-once, no ack, no replay, and no durable message queue, and the docs say so plainly. As a scripter who audits state from the outside, I confirmed the BEHAVIOR matches the CLAIMS across five independent probes (no src reading):

1) CLI surface: no `ack`/`replay` subcommands; `--help` exposes nothing about semantics (honesty is docs-only). 
2) Retained/replay: a publish before any subscriber, and three publishes while a subscriber is offline, leave NOTHING recoverable — `find $STATE -type f` shows only broker.log + a 0-byte .drops.lock. The reconnected subscriber recovers zero, then receives a fresh live publish correctly. No retained, no replay. 
3) Deferred files are a pending-DEADLINE store, not a message queue: a `--delay` with no subscriber fires into the void (deferred file consumed, nothing held), a late subscriber gets nothing, and with a sub online it fires exactly once. 
4) Modulo-crash corner: only the deferred record survives kill -9; it re-arms on restart and fires exactly once (occurrences:1). Ordinary messages leave nothing across a crash. This is precisely 'at-most-once-modulo-crash'. 
5) No retry-until-acked: 200 publishes to a healthy sub all delivered with `deferred/` empty throughout (no retry queue), pub-to-empty is a clean exit=0 no-op, and the shipped flood_wedged.sh shows overload is COUNTED-and-DROPPED (drops_*=354), never retried/buffered.

The user is NOT misled. The strongest honesty signal is the filesystem-as-API itself: there is simply no place on disk for a missed message to hide.

The friction is around DISCOVERABILITY of this honesty, not the honesty itself: (a) the binary's `--help` says nothing about semantics — the caveat lives only in DEMO.md's footer; (b) README's status line advertises 'crash recovery' before the 'at-most-once' qualifier appears (and only in DEMO.md), which a fast reader could over-read; (c) `sub --help`/`pub --help` crash with `$2: unbound variable` (lines 323/342) instead of printing usage — an ugly CLI rough edge. None of these mislead about delivery guarantees; they just make the (correct) story harder to find from the tool itself. Recommend surfacing a one-line 'at-most-once; connect-before-publish; no replay/ack' in `--help` and near the top of README.

NOTE: I did not run chaos_deadline.sh this session (out of scope for the at-least-once probe), so I make no claim on the missed=0/dup=0/negative-control axis here; I only observed the deferred scheduler's user-facing crash-recovery and fire-once behavior, which were correct.

Scratch scripts I created were removed (atleastonce_*.sh); other files under .eval-scratch/ belong to other personas and were left untouched.
